steps.title: The Lot That Wasn't
steps.narrative_intro: Your phone buzzes three times in rapid succession. The first message is from your client — the auction house — timestamped 41 minutes ago: "Where are you? Lot 19 sold. Call us." The second is from an unknown French number: a single photograph of a reliquary sitting on black velvet, its base partially unscrewed, a yellowed parchment visible inside. No text. The third is a voice note from a woman who speaks like someone accustomed to being listened to: "You're the authenticator. You were supposed to be here before the hammer fell. You weren't. That's either incompetence or a choice, and I don't have time to care which. The buyer is at the Carlton. The reliquary is already in a case. You have a window — a very small window — before it goes into a car and then onto a boat. I'm sending you a partial image of the parchment they found inside. Look at it. Then walk to the Carlton and photograph the east-wing service entrance on Rue du Commandant André. Tell me what vehicles you see parked there. Do it now. Not in five minutes. Now." The voice belongs to Vera Solari, and she has already decided you're useful.
steps.instruction: Walk to the Carlton InterContinental on the Croisette. When you arrive, move to the east side of the building along Rue du Commandant André and photograph the service entrance area. Vera needs to know what vehicles are staged there — she believes the buyer's transport team is using the service entrance to avoid the lobby. Send her the photo through the app. While you wait for her response, examine the partial parchment image she sent you: count the number of visible symbols along the left margin and report that number to her. This is your first authentication task — prove you're worth keeping in the loop.
steps.player_action: Photograph the Carlton's service entrance / side street area on Rue du Commandant André, then examine the parchment document in your vault and count the symbols along the left margin. Report both the photo and the count to Vera.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution partial image of the cipher parchment found inside the reliquary base appears in the player's vault. The parchment shows faded ink markings — a grid of symbols along the left margin (7 distinct symbols), partial coastline drawing resembling the Îles de Lérins, and Latin text fragments referencing 'custos carceris' (keeper of the prison). A handwritten note from Vera is pinned to it: 'This is all I could photograph before they closed the case. The symbols are a substitution cipher — 17th-century Provençal monastic notation. You should recognize it if you're half as good as your reputation. Count the margin symbols. I need to know if this matches what I already have.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the side/service entrance area of the Carlton hotel. AI identifies the iconic Belle Époque facade, any vehicles (delivery vans, luxury cars, SUVs with tinted windows), the service door, and surrounding street details. The AI character Vera interprets what she sees: specific vehicles become 'the buyer's extraction setup,' architectural details become surveillance reference points. If the player captures luxury vehicles or dark SUVs, Vera reacts with urgency. If the street is empty, Vera recalibrates — 'They've already moved. Faster than I expected.'

tension.description: The buyer's team is allegedly staging transport at the Carlton's service entrance. Vera implies the reliquary is about to be moved to the Vieux Port yacht. The player must act fast — but has no way to verify if the urgency is real or manufactured by Vera to control them.
tension.resolution: Whether the player acts quickly or slowly, the reliquary has already left the Carlton by the time they arrive. But the photo they take and the symbol count they provide determine whether Vera trusts them enough to share her real objective.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo of the Carlton service entrance
character_interactions.phone_version: Vera responds within 30 seconds, clipped and analytical: 'Two vehicles. The black Viano — that's private security, Meridian Group, they handle transport for half the auctions on the Côte. The grey Audi is more interesting. No plates visible. That's the buyer's people. You're too late for interception but not too late to matter. Now — the parchment. How many symbols on the left margin? Don't guess. Count.' If the street is empty: 'Nothing. They moved early. Someone tipped them. This changes the geometry. The symbols — how many on the left margin? I need your count before I decide what to tell you next.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's voice cuts through with zero warmth: 'I can see what you're looking at. Two vehicles — or none. Either way, they're ahead of us. Focus on what's in your vault. The parchment. Left margin. Count the symbols and say the number out loud. I need to hear you think before I trust you with what comes next.'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes the Carlton service entrance in real-time, identifying vehicles, doors, and architectural features Vera can reference

character_interactions.trigger: 90 seconds after player sends photo to Vera, regardless of response
character_interactions.phone_version: A new unknown number messages the player. No name, just a question: 'You were hired to authenticate Lot 19. Who hired you — the auction house, or someone else?' Then, 10 seconds later: 'I was at the auction. I saw the parchment before the base was sealed. There are seven symbols on the left margin — but one of them is wrong. It doesn't belong to any 17th-century Provençal cipher I've studied, and I've studied them all. My name is Noor Kazan. My father was Dr. Farid Kazan, University of Marseille. If that name means anything to you, we should talk. If it doesn't, look him up. Then we should talk.'

rayban_version.script: A calm, precise voice — younger than Vera, with a faint accent that could be Turkish or Lebanese: 'I don't know who you're working with yet. But I was at the auction. I saw the parchment. Seven symbols on the margin, and one of them is a fake. That matters more than you realize right now. My name is Noor Kazan. Look up my father — Dr. Farid Kazan, Marseille. Then decide if you want to hear what I know.'

character_interactions.trigger: Player reports the symbol count to Vera (any number)
character_interactions.phone_version: An anonymous message arrives — no number, no sender ID, routed through the app's encrypted channel. Just text: 'The reliquary is Toulon workshop. 1884. Not 17th century. The parchment is older than the box. Ask yourself why.' No option to reply. The message self-marks as read after 30 seconds.

rayban_version.script: A digitally distorted voice, gender-neutral, flat affect: 'The reliquary. Toulon workshop. Eighteen eighty-four. The parchment is older than the box. Ask yourself why.' The message ends. No reply option.

verification.target: Carlton hotel service entrance / side street on Rue du Commandant André
verification.success_condition: Player submits a photo showing the Carlton building's side/service area — AI recognizes the Belle Époque facade, street-level details, or any vehicles present
verification.success_reaction: Vera responds with increased trust: 'Good eye. You notice details — that's rare in people who show up late. I'm sending you coordinates for your next stop. The reliquary left the Carlton nine minutes ago heading toward the Vieux Port, but there's a stop first. The buyer's appraiser is working out of a place near the Marché Forville. I need you there before they finish their own authentication. Move.' Player receives +15 XP and a second vault document: a partial auction manifest showing Lot 19's provenance chain with suspicious gaps.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player sends an unrelated photo or doesn't send one, Vera messages: 'I asked for the service entrance, not a postcard. Fine. I don't need your eyes — I need your expertise. The reliquary is already gone from the Carlton. I'm sending you to the next location. Don't waste my time twice.' The story continues with slightly lower trust from Vera, but the player still receives the next location.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 5 minutes with no photo, Vera sends: 'You're either careful or frozen. Either way, I can't wait. The reliquary left the Carlton. I'm sending coordinates — Marché Forville area. The buyer's appraiser is there. Get moving or I find someone else. I always have someone else.'

steps.unlock_message: A partial image of the cipher parchment has appeared in your vault. Vera Solari is watching your response time.

steps.title: The Jailer's Geometry

activity.name: La Croisette Waterfront — The Palais des Festivals Esplanade

steps.narrative_intro: You've barely left the Carlton's shadow when your phone buzzes three times in rapid succession — three different senders. Vera's message is terse: "The cipher map uses a 17th-century surveyor's grid. The key is angular orientation — the jailer was a military cartographer before he was a prison guard. Find me a sightline." Marco's is panicked: "Someone at the auction photographed us both. I'm sending you the partial image of the map the buyer's broker flashed during bidding. Study it. Tell me what projection system it uses — I need to know if this thing is real before I do something I can't undo." And then a number you don't recognize sends a single line: "The Palais steps face 187° south-southwest. That matters. — Le Pharmacien." You're walking toward the Palais des Festivals now, where the MIPIM detritus still litters the esplanade — abandoned banner frames, cable runs taped to concrete, the skeletal remains of a conference that moved billions. Somewhere in this architectural wreckage, three people are waiting for you to confirm whether a 328-year-old map is authentic. The problem is, you're starting to suspect each of them already knows the answer.
steps.instruction: Head to the Palais des Festivals esplanade (3-minute walk east along the Croisette from the Carlton). When you arrive, Marco will send you a partial photograph of the cipher map shown during the auction. Your job: study the real architectural orientation of the Palais des Festivals — specifically the famous front steps facing the sea — and compare the angles to the map fragment. Photograph the Palais steps from the waterfront side, capturing the full staircase and its orientation toward the harbor. Send the photo to Vera. Then tell Marco what compass bearing the steps face and whether the map's grid lines could use the Palais (or its historical predecessor, the old Casino Municipal) as an orientation anchor.
steps.player_action: Photograph the grand staircase of the Palais des Festivals from the waterfront, capturing the full width of the steps and their orientation toward the Vieux Port. Then analyze the partial map image Marco sends and report to him what cartographic projection or grid system the map appears to use, based on what you observe about directional alignments in the real architecture.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution crop of the cipher map fragment — showing a hand-drawn grid of intersecting lines overlaid on what appears to be a coastal outline, with tiny annotations in Old French. Three buildings are marked with crosses. Angular measurements are scratched in the margins. A watermark is faintly visible: the Lérins abbey seal. Marco's accompanying message reads: 'This is all I got before the broker locked the display case. The angles — they're not random. Someone used real buildings as reference points. I need you to tell me which ones.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the grand staircase of the Palais des Festivals from the waterfront/beach side. AI should identify: the iconic red-carpet steps, the modernist concrete facade, the orientation of the building toward the sea and Vieux Port, any visible compass directions or harbor landmarks. Narratively, the AI interprets the architectural angles as matching — or contradicting — the grid lines on the cipher map fragment, noting that the Palais sits on the exact site of the old Casino Municipal which would have existed in the 17th century as a coastal landmark visible from Île Sainte-Marguerite.

tension.description: Three characters are simultaneously demanding the player's expertise on the cipher map's authenticity. Each will interpret the player's analysis differently — Vera needs confirmation to advance her trap, Marco needs reassurance before committing to a dangerous play, and Le Pharmacien is testing whether the player actually has the skills they claim. The player's assessment here determines how much trust each character extends for the rest of the quest.
tension.resolution: The player's photo and analysis — accurate or not — triggers divergent character reactions. A strong analysis earns Marco's vulnerable honesty and Le Pharmacien's grudging respect. A weak one causes Vera to start withholding information and Marco to spiral into recklessness.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends analysis of the map's grid system after photographing the Palais steps
character_interactions.phone_version: Marco responds with voice message, audibly shaken: 'A triangulation grid. Using coastal landmarks. God, that's — that's exactly what Noor's father published in his last paper before the university buried him. The Lérins cartographic method. She was right. Her father was right.' Long pause. 'Listen, I need to tell you something about Noor Kazan. We... knew each other. Istanbul, 2019. She was researching Ottoman trade routes and I was — it doesn't matter what I was doing. The point is, if she's here and she sees that map, she'll recognize the methodology instantly. And she'll know I'm the one who stole her father's research notes from the Istanbul archive. Do NOT mention my name to her. Please.'

rayban_version.script: Marco's voice crackles through, strained and rapid: 'Triangulation grid. Coastal landmarks. That's the Lérins method — the same system Noor Kazan's father described before they discredited him. She was right all along. Listen — I knew Noor. Istanbul, 2019. If she sees that map, she'll know I stole her father's research from the archive. Do not mention my name to her. I'm begging you.'

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo of Palais des Festivals steps
character_interactions.phone_version: Vera texts back within 90 seconds — unnervingly fast, as if she was watching for the upload: 'Good. The steps face 187° SSW — Le Pharmacien wasn't wrong about that, which irritates me. Now look at the map fragment Marco sent you. See the longest line, the one cutting diagonally from the upper cross to the coastal outline? That's the sightline from Fort Royal on Sainte-Marguerite to the old Casino Municipal — which stood exactly where you're standing now. The jailer used buildings he could SEE from the prison as his grid anchors. Which means the map's destination point can be reverse-calculated from those angles. I've already done the math. It points to Le Suquet — specifically, to a wall inside what is now the Musée des Explorations du Monde. I need you to get there. But first, I need to know: did Marco tell you about Istanbul?'

rayban_version.script: Vera's voice, clipped and controlled: 'The steps face 187° south-southwest. Le Pharmacien was right, which irritates me. Now — the longest line on that map fragment runs from Fort Royal to the old Casino Municipal. You're standing on its exact site. The jailer used visible coastal landmarks as his grid. I've reverse-calculated the angles. They point to Le Suquet — a wall inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde. We need to get there. But first: did Marco tell you about Istanbul?'

character_interactions.trigger: GPS arrival at Palais des Festivals esplanade, delayed 4 minutes after arrival
character_interactions.phone_version: A message from the unknown number, no greeting: 'If the grid is triangulated from Fort Royal, the margin error at this distance is ±3 meters per degree of miscalculation. A 17th-century compass had ±2° variance. That means the map's destination has a search radius of roughly 6 meters. Enough to identify a wall. Not enough to identify a stone. You'll need the full map for that. The buyer has the full map. The buyer is on a yacht called the Seraphim Star. The Seraphim Star is registered to a shell company in Cyprus that also owns four apartments in the Gray d'Albion building. The Gray d'Albion apartments were purchased with proceeds from jewels stolen from the Carlton InterContinental on July 28, 2013. This is not a treasure hunt. This is a crime scene. Act accordingly. — Le Pharmacien'

rayban_version.script: Flat, synthetic-sounding voice — possibly run through a modulator: 'Triangulated grid from Fort Royal. Margin error: plus or minus three meters per degree. Seventeenth-century compass variance: two degrees. Search radius at destination: six meters. Enough for a wall. Not a stone. You need the full map. The buyer has it. The yacht is the Seraphim Star. Registered to a Cyprus shell. Same shell that owns Gray d'Albion apartments. Purchased with Carlton heist proceeds. July 28, 2013. This is a crime scene. Act accordingly.'

verification.target: Grand staircase of the Palais des Festivals, captured from the waterfront/Croisette side showing full steps and sea-facing orientation
verification.success_condition: AI identifies the Palais des Festivals iconic staircase, its south-southwest orientation, and the player provides a plausible cartographic analysis (mentioning triangulation, grid orientation, coastal landmarks, or angular measurement)
verification.success_reaction: Vera sends a follow-up: 'You actually know what you're doing. That changes things. I was prepared to feed you simplified instructions, but you don't need training wheels. Here — ' She unlocks a second image in the vault: a high-resolution scan of a 1687 military survey of Cannes showing the exact buildings that would have been visible from Fort Royal. 'Cross-reference this with the map fragment. You'll see the third anchor point. It's the church tower in Le Suquet.' +40 XP. Marco's trust increases — he sends a personal detail: 'My real name isn't Delvecchio. But you've earned the right to not hear that story yet.'
verification.failure_fallback: Vera texts: 'The photo's usable but your analysis is thin. I'll do the cartographic work — you focus on getting to Le Suquet. I'm sending you simplified coordinates.' She sends a marked-up version of the map fragment with arrows and annotations, effectively doing the work for the player. Story continues with slightly reduced trust from Marco, who texts: 'Not sure you're the expert they said you were. But we're past the point of finding someone else.' Le Pharmacien sends nothing — silence is his judgment.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 6 minutes, Marco sends an anxious text: 'Are you still at the Palais? The yacht crew just started engine checks — that's a 40-minute pre-departure sequence. We're running out of margin. Send me ANYTHING — even a guess. I'll take a guess over silence right now.' If no response after 8 minutes, Vera intervenes: 'I don't have time to wait for perfectionism. I've done my own triangulation. Get to the Forville Market area — I'll direct you from there.'

steps.unlock_message: Marco's partial map fragment has been added to your vault. Three sightlines. Three anchor points. The geometry of a 328-year-old prison is still visible in the streets beneath your feet.

steps.title: The Pharmacist's Price

activity.name: Vieux Port de Cannes — Quai Saint-Pierre Investigation

steps.narrative_intro:
The esplanade geometry gave you three coordinates. Two match locations on the modern Cannes grid. The third doesn't exist — it points to open water, roughly where a 68-meter seized superyacht named *Seraphim* has been anchored in the Vieux Port since February, frozen by a French court order tied to EU sanctions. Skeleton crew. Legal limbo. And according to Vera, the anonymous buyer's extraction vehicle.

But as you cut south toward the old port, your phone buzzes twice in rapid succession. The first message is from a number you don't recognize — a single image: a pharmaceutical label from 1887, handwritten in Latin, with the word PERSONA scratched into the margin. Le Pharmacien has found you.

The second message is from Noor Kazan. She's tracked the same coordinates you have. She wants to collaborate — pool what you've decoded from the esplanade with what she pulled from the auction's provenance files. She's offering her father's research on the Iron Mask jailer's cipher system in exchange. But she wants to meet you at the port. Not in person — she'll be watching the yacht from somewhere along the quay, and she needs your eyes on something she can't see from her position.

The Vieux Port is busy. Fishing boats, charter yachts, the ferry terminal to the Lérins Islands. Somewhere along Quai Saint-Pierre, between the fish market and the harbor master's office, you need to find the *Seraphim* — or at least confirm it's really there. Because Le Pharmacien's message implies something Vera hasn't told you: the yacht may have already moved once this week.
steps.instruction: Head to Quai Saint-Pierre at the Vieux Port. When you arrive, locate and photograph any large yacht or vessel moored in the port — Noor needs visual confirmation of what's anchored there. While you photograph, count the number of visible surveillance cameras along the quay between the fish market stalls and the port authority building. Report the count to Le Pharmacien — he claims it will tell him whether the port's security grid has been "adjusted" for the buyer's departure. This is a collaborative step: share your camera feed and findings with your quest partner to cross-reference observations.
steps.player_action: Photograph the largest yacht or vessel visible in the Vieux Port from Quai Saint-Pierre and send it to Noor. Then count the surveillance cameras visible along the quay between the fish market area and the port authority office, and report the number to Le Pharmacien.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A scanned image appears in the vault: a 19th-century pharmaceutical inventory page from a Cannes apothecary, with three entries circled in red ink. The circled entries are all compounds used in period-accurate iron gall ink — the same ink type that would have been used for any 1698 document. A handwritten annotation in modern pen reads: 'She mixed it herself. Ask her which century.' This is Le Pharmacien's first direct accusation that Vera forged the cipher map.

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs a yacht or large vessel in the Vieux Port of Cannes. AI interprets the vessel's details — size, flag, deck equipment, any visible name — and Noor reacts narratively, comparing what she sees to the known profile of the seized superyacht Seraphim. If the yacht matches (large, white, multiple decks), Noor confirms the extraction vehicle is still in port. If it's a different vessel, Noor notes the Seraphim may have been moved to the outer anchorage — raising the stakes on the timeline.

tension.description: Noor and Le Pharmacien both want the player's observations but for opposing reasons. Noor needs yacht confirmation to time her own move on the reliquary. Le Pharmacien is building a case that Vera's entire operation is a fabrication — and the camera count is his way of testing whether the player will share raw intelligence with him or filter it through Vera first. Sharing with both means neither fully trusts you. Choosing one burns the other.
tension.resolution: The player's choice of who receives the most detailed information shifts alliance dynamics. Le Pharmacien rewards honesty with a critical revelation about the ink forgery. Noor rewards cooperation with her father's cipher key fragment. Both paths advance the story but reconfigure trust.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo of yacht/vessel in the port
character_interactions.phone_version: Noor responds with a voice message, her tone measured but urgent: 'That's... not the Seraphim. Or it's been repainted — the Seraphim had a dark blue hull when my contact photographed it in Monaco last month. Either they've disguised it or Vera's been lying about the extraction timeline. I'm sending you a fragment of my father's cipher analysis — he spent eleven years on the Iron Mask correspondence before the university stripped his tenure. Page 7 is the one that matters. The jailer used a rotating key based on liturgical calendar dates. If the reliquary map is authentic, the cipher resets on saint's days. If it doesn't... it's modern. Check it against what Vera gave you.' A 3-page academic PDF appears in the vault.

rayban_version.script: Noor's voice comes through your frames, low and controlled, as if she's speaking from somewhere nearby but refuses to say where. 'Look at the hull color. Does that match a vessel that's been sitting in legal seizure since February? Paint oxidizes. Salt air does things. If that hull is pristine, someone's maintaining it — or it arrived recently. Send me the photo. I'm giving you my father's cipher work in exchange. Page seven. The liturgical key. It's how we prove whether Vera's map is real or manufactured.'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes the photographed vessel's hull condition, size, visible flags or registration, and deck configuration to determine if it matches the profile of a seized oligarch yacht

character_interactions.trigger: Player reports camera count or sends any observation about port security
character_interactions.phone_version: A text message, no greeting: 'Fourteen was the number last Tuesday. If you counted fewer, they've been disabled. If more, new ones were installed for the departure. Either way, someone with port authority access is facilitating. — You found the document I left in your vault. The ink compounds. Vera Solari purchased iron gall ink reagents from a specialty supplier in Grasse eight weeks ago. I have the receipt. She built that cipher map in her apartment. The question isn't whether it's a forgery. The question is whether what the forgery points to is still real. Ask yourself: why would she forge a map to a location that contains nothing?' A purchase receipt image appears in the vault.

rayban_version.script: Le Pharmacien's voice is synthetic, digitally altered, gender-indeterminate. A whisper that sounds like it's being read from notes. 'The cameras. How many. This matters more than you think. Port surveillance in Cannes runs through a municipal system that was upgraded after twenty-fifteen. Any deviation from baseline means someone paid for a gap. Count carefully. And while you're counting — did you read what I sent you? The ink compounds. She bought them in Grasse. Eight weeks ago. Vera made that map herself. But here is what I cannot determine from where I sit: does the location the map points to actually contain something? That is why I need you. You are my eyes in the geometry.'

character_interactions.trigger: Time elapsed: 6 minutes after GPS arrival, or immediately if player shares Le Pharmacien's accusations
character_interactions.phone_version: Vera calls. She sounds like she's walking fast, slightly out of breath. 'I know Le Pharmacien contacted you. I know what he sent. He's not wrong about the ink — I'm not going to insult you by denying it. Yes. I drew the map. But I drew it from memory, Player. I saw the original in 2019, in a private collection in Beirut, for eleven seconds before the owner's security removed me from the room. Eleven seconds. I have an eidetic memory for spatial layouts — it's why I was good at what I did before all this. The map I recreated is accurate to within millimeters. The forgery is the medium, not the content. What's inside that museum is real. And if you let Le Pharmacien's obsession with process destroy the only chance we have of reaching it before the buyer's network — then we all lose. Including Noor's father, who deserved better than what the academic establishment did to him. You have the cipher key fragment from her now. Use it. The next location is in Le Suquet. I need you to trust me for twenty more minutes.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's voice cuts in, raw, no preamble, the sound of wind and traffic behind her. 'Stop. Before you decide I'm the villain — yes. I made the map. The physical object is mine. But the information on it is real. I saw the original for eleven seconds in Beirut in twenty-nineteen. I have eidetic spatial memory. That is not a boast, it is a diagnosis. What I drew is accurate. Le Pharmacien wants to discredit me because I wouldn't share my source with him three years ago. He's brilliant and he's petty and he's not wrong about the ink and he's completely wrong about what matters. The documents inside that museum are real. Noor's father knew it. Use her cipher key. Get to Le Suquet. Trust me for twenty more minutes. That's all I'm asking.'

verification.target: Yacht/vessel photo sent to Noor + surveillance camera count sent to Le Pharmacien
verification.success_condition: Player sends a clear photo of a vessel in the Vieux Port AND provides a specific number for camera count
verification.success_reaction: Both Noor and Le Pharmacien reward the player with critical intel. Noor's cipher fragment unlocks the liturgical key needed to validate the map. Le Pharmacien's ink receipt proves Vera's forgery but his final question — 'why forge a map to nothing?' — plants the seed that Vera may be telling the truth about the underlying discovery. Player gains trust with both characters and receives +40 XP. Vera's subsequent call feels more desperate and honest because the player now holds leverage.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player sends only the photo or only the count, the character who received nothing sends a curt message: Noor says 'I gave you my father's work. You gave me silence. Fine. But you'll need page seven eventually.' Le Pharmacien says 'Incomplete data is worse than no data. I'll assume you're working for her now.' The missing intel piece is provided anyway after 3 minutes via a 'consolation' message from the overlooked character, but trust is reduced. Story continues to Step 4.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 8 minutes, Marco Delvecchio sends an unsolicited message: 'Still at the port? Bad idea to linger. The harbor master's office logs foot traffic near restricted berths. I can see you've been stationary too long on the GPS. Move toward Le Suquet. I'll explain when you're walking.' This nudges the player toward Step 4 while adding Marco's surveillance layer to the tension.

steps.unlock_message: A pharmaceutical inventory from 1887 has appeared in your vault. Someone called Le Pharmacien wants you to see what Vera has been buying.

steps.title: The Iron Saint's Face

activity.name: Le Suquet — Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance Facade Investigation
activity.address: 1 Place de la Castre, 06400 Cannes, France

steps.narrative_intro:
The climb up Le Suquet feels like leaving one century for another. The narrow streets tighten around you — limestone walls, shuttered windows, laundry lines casting shadows like prison bars. Your calves burn. Good. Pain means you're still ahead of whoever else is climbing.

Le Pharmacien's coordinates didn't lead to a building. They led to a face. Specifically, to the carved stone facade of Notre-Dame d'Espérance, the church that has watched over Cannes since 1648 — thirty years before the Man in the Iron Mask was moved from Île Sainte-Marguerite. The cipher map's second reference point isn't a street or a monument. It's an architectural detail on this church. A saint's face. According to Le Pharmacien's fragment, the jailer encoded the next bearing using the direction a particular carved figure is looking.

But your phone buzzes as you climb. It's Noor. She sounds different now — urgent, the intellectual coolness cracking. She says Marco Delvecchio just contacted the buyer's network directly. She intercepted the message. He's offering to authenticate the reliquary himself — cutting you out entirely. He's telling them you're compromised. She wants to know: did you share anything with Capitaine Morin?

You haven't even caught your breath.
steps.instruction: Climb to the Place de la Castre at the top of Le Suquet. Find the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance. Photograph the carved stone figures above the main entrance — focus on any saint or figure whose face is turned to one side rather than facing forward. Send the photo to Noor, who claims she can cross-reference the figure's gaze direction with the cipher map fragment. But be aware: Vera will also contact you, and her instructions will contradict Noor's.
steps.player_action: Photograph the carved stone figures on the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance, specifically any figure whose head or gaze is turned to one side. Examine the direction the figure faces and report it (left, right, upward) to the characters who request it.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution scan of a 1698 prison inventory from Île Sainte-Marguerite appears in the vault. One entry is circled in red digital ink: 'Une figure de saint, en pierre, transportée de l'île à la chapelle du Suquet, sur ordre du gouverneur.' A stone saint figure, transported from the island to the Le Suquet chapel on the governor's order. Le Pharmacien's annotation reads: 'The jailer didn't draw a map. He built one. Into the city itself. The saint's gaze is the compass bearing. —Ph.' Below is a partial overlay showing how three reference points (the Carlton's original site, the port, and this church) form a triangle whose center falls inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde, 90 meters from where you stand.

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the stone carvings on the facade of Notre-Dame d'Espérance. AI should identify carved figures, saints, or reliefs above the entrance. Narratively interpret the direction any figure's gaze points as a compass bearing that, combined with previous reference points (the Carlton and the Vieux Port), triangulates a specific room inside the nearby Musée des Explorations du Monde. If the photo shows the church clearly, confirm the bearing. If unclear or wrong building, have Noor express doubt and ask for another angle.

tension.description: Marco has gone directly to the buyer's network, offering to replace the player as authenticator. Noor reveals this but her motives are suspect — she may be manufacturing urgency to control the player's next move. Simultaneously, Vera contacts the player with a contradictory instruction: do NOT send the photo to Noor, send it only to her. The player must choose who to trust with the critical compass bearing, knowing that sharing it determines who reaches the Musée first.
tension.resolution: The player sends the photo to one or both parties. If they send to Noor, she provides a detailed but slightly altered bearing (protecting her own interest in reaching the documents first). If they send to Vera, she gives the accurate bearing but reveals nothing about why she needs it so badly. If they send to both, the contradictions between their interpretations become visible — a clue that one of them is lying. The story continues regardless, but the player's information flow determines alliance positioning for the climax.

character_interactions.trigger: GPS arrival at Place de la Castre
character_interactions.phone_version: Noor calls immediately, voice tight with controlled anger: 'I intercepted a message from Delvecchio to the buyer's broker. He's telling them you've been talking to the police. He's offering himself as replacement authenticator. That snake — he did the same thing in Istanbul.' She catches herself. Pauses. 'Forget I said that. Listen, I need you to photograph the carved figures on the church facade. My father's research predicted that the jailer used architectural landmarks as cipher points. The direction the saint faces gives us the final bearing. Send me the photo. Only me. Can you do that?' If the player asks about Istanbul or Marco, she deflects: 'Later. We're losing time. The bearing first.'

rayban_version.script: Noor's voice comes through the glasses, clipped and urgent: 'Delvecchio just betrayed you to the buyer's network. He's offering to replace you. I intercepted the message. I need you to look at the church facade — the carved figures above the entrance. Find the one whose face is turned. That's our compass point. My father spent years on this. Describe what you see.'

camera_mode.purpose: Player looks at the church facade through Ray-Ban camera; AI identifies carved figures and their gaze direction, feeding this into Noor's real-time analysis

character_interactions.phone_version: A text from Vera, all caps: 'DO NOT SEND THAT PHOTO TO NOOR. She is not who she says she is. Her father didn't just study the Iron Mask — he was hired by the same network that bought the reliquary. His research was funded by laundered money from the 2013 heist. She's not seeking academic vindication. She's completing a transaction her father started. Send the photo to me. I will explain everything at the next location. You have earned that much.' A pause. Then: 'I know I'm asking you to trust me. I know I haven't given you enough reason. But Noor has given you less. Think about what she's actually told you versus what she's asked you to do.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's message appears as text overlay in the glasses: 'STOP. Do not share the facade photo with Noor. Her father was funded by the same network behind the Carlton heist. She is finishing his transaction, not his research. Send the bearing to me. I will explain at the museum. You have earned that.'

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo to either Noor or Vera (or 3 minutes after arrival if no action)
character_interactions.phone_version: A terse audio message from Morin: 'This is Capitaine Morin. I'll be brief because I don't know who else is listening. Delvecchio was just seen at the Palais des Festivals talking to a man matching the description of the buyer's broker. He had documents. If your so-called colleagues are telling you to rush, ask yourself who benefits from you not stopping to think. The yacht hasn't moved. Its engine hasn't started. Someone is lying to you about the clock. I don't need your photo. I need you to keep your eyes open at the museum. Morin out.'

rayban_version.script: Morin's voice, low and steady through the glasses: 'Capitaine Morin. Delvecchio was seen at the Palais talking to the buyer's broker. He had documents. Your friends are pushing you to rush — ask who benefits. The yacht hasn't moved. Someone is lying about the deadline. I don't need your photo. I need your eyes at the museum. Morin out.'

verification.target: Carved stone figures on the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance, focusing on gaze direction
verification.success_condition: Photo clearly shows the church facade with identifiable carved figures or architectural reliefs. AI can determine which direction a figure's head or gaze is oriented.
verification.success_reaction: Whichever character receives the photo responds with a triangulation analysis. The three reference points — the Carlton (Step 1), the Vieux Port (Step 3), and the saint's gaze bearing — converge on a specific wing of the Musée des Explorations du Monde. The player receives +15 XP and an exclusive vault document: a superimposed map showing the triangulation with a red circle marking the exact room. The character who received the photo addresses the player with new respect: 'You see it now, don't you? The jailer hid the answer in plain sight for three hundred years. And you just read it.'
verification.failure_fallback: If the photo is unclear or of the wrong building, Noor (or Vera) expresses frustration but pivots: 'The angle isn't right, but it doesn't matter — Le Pharmacien's overlay already narrowed it to the museum's west gallery. We go with what we have. Move to the Musée now.' The player still progresses but without the precise room location, making the next step slightly harder. Dimitri sends a mocking voice note: 'Couldn't find the church? It's the big stone thing. With the cross. On top of the hill you're standing on.'
verification.timeout_reaction: After 5 minutes of inaction, Dimitri sends a message: 'Standing on a hilltop staring at a church. Very spiritual. Very useless. Delvecchio is already at the Palais selling your credentials to the buyer. Move or be moved.' Simultaneously, Vera sends coordinates for the museum directly: 'Forget the bearing. I have enough. Meet me at the next point. We're out of time — or we're not. Either way, standing still is the one thing that definitely doesn't help.'

steps.unlock_message: A 1698 prison inventory document has appeared in your vault. Le Pharmacien has marked an entry about a stone saint transported from Île Sainte-Marguerite to Le Suquet. The jailer didn't just draw a map — he built it into the architecture of Cannes itself.

steps.title: The Mask Behind the Mask

activity.name: Musée des Explorations du Monde — Exterior Investigation & Cipher Decode
activity.address: Place de la Castre, Le Suquet, 06400 Cannes, France

steps.narrative_intro: The climb up to the Castre plateau has left your calves burning, but it's your chest that's tight. You're standing where history locked a man away and erased his name so thoroughly that three centuries of scholars couldn't recover it. The Musée des Explorations du Monde — housed inside the medieval castle of the Cannes monks — sits before you, its 11th-century tower rising like a finger pressed to stone lips. Somewhere inside these walls, according to the cipher map you've been chasing, a set of documents was sealed behind masonry during renovations in 1698, the same month the Iron Mask prisoner was transferred to the Bastille. But Vera's map is a forgery. You know that now. Which means either the documents don't exist at all... or Vera reverse-engineered her fake from something real. The museum's exterior holds the answer. The original castle walls bear mason's marks — stonemason signatures carved during medieval construction and again during 17th-century restorations. If the cipher map's coordinate system matches the actual pattern of mason's marks on the tower, Vera didn't invent the map. She copied it from a source she hasn't told you about. Your expertise as an authenticator comes down to this: read the stones.
steps.instruction: Make your way to the base of the Tour du Suquet (the square medieval tower beside the Musée des Explorations du Monde). Photograph the tower's stone facade, focusing on the lower sections where original mason's marks and restoration seams are visible. Count the distinct mason's marks you can identify on the visible face of the tower and report your findings to Noor. Then photograph the panoramic view of the Vieux Port from the Castre esplanade — you need to confirm whether the seized yacht Vera described is actually visible at the quay.
steps.player_action: Photograph the stone facade of the Tour du Suquet tower, identify and count visible mason's marks or carved symbols on the lower stones, then photograph the panoramic view of the Vieux Port below to verify the presence of a large yacht at the quay. Report both findings to Noor via the app.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution scan of a 1698 inventory ledger from the Lérins monastery archives, listing 'one reliquary, brass and bone, contents sealed by order of M. de Saint-Mars' — the real jailer of the Iron Mask. A margin note in different ink reads: 'The marks on the Castre tower correspond. Third face, seventh course.' Le Pharmacien has annotated the scan: 'Vera found this ledger in 2019. She never told anyone. Now you know where her map came from. The question is what she ADDED to the original. —P'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the medieval Tour du Suquet tower facade. AI should identify the old stone construction, any visible carved marks or symbols in the stonework, restoration seams between different eras of masonry, and the general condition of the medieval walls. The AI character (Noor) will interpret these marks as potential cipher reference points, comparing them to the map's coordinate system. Second photo: panoramic view of the Vieux Port from above — AI identifies boats, yachts, the quay layout, and any notably large vessel that could be the seized oligarch yacht.

tension.description: Three truths collide simultaneously: Le Pharmacien's leaked document proves Vera based her forgery on a real 1698 source, Noor realizes the player now has enough information to authenticate OR discredit the entire cipher map, and Yael Morin demands the player share everything before the buyer's yacht departs. The player must decide whose version of events to validate — and every choice closes a door permanently.
tension.resolution: The player's analysis of the mason's marks and their report to Noor determines whether the historical documents are treated as real (triggering a race to the final location) or fabricated (triggering a confrontation with Vera). Either path leads to Step 6, but the alliances are radically different.

[pause] I need you to understand something. Whatever you find in Step 6 — the historical documents, the evidence — I don't care about the Carlton network or Vera's justice crusade. verification.target: Mason's marks on Tour du Suquet facade + panoramic port view with yacht identification
verification.success_condition: Player photographs the tower showing visible carved marks or stone patterns AND provides a count of marks (any reasonable number between 5-20) AND photographs the port panorama showing vessels at quay
verification.success_reaction: Noor confirms the player's count aligns closely with her father's 2003 survey, granting +30 trust with Noor and unlocking her father's complete field notes in the vault — a 47-page document that contains the REAL cipher key, independent of Vera's reconstruction. This gives the player an authentication advantage in Step 6. Noor whispers: 'You just did what an entire review board refused to do. You confirmed his work. I won't forget this.' Yael Morin also gains +15 trust if the player shared the port photo with her, as she can now dispatch harbor police.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player cannot identify mason's marks or provides no count, Noor is disappointed but sends her father's field notes anyway with a colder tone: 'Fine. The marks are subtle — my father needed specialized lighting to document most of them. I'll send you his survey. Use it in Step 6. But I needed YOU to see them. Not just read about them.' The story continues to Step 6 with reduced trust from Noor (-15) but the same destination.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 6 minutes, Dimitri sends an uncharacteristic message: '⏱️ The aft tender just hit the water. Move or don't. Your choice has a shelf life.' Vera follows 30 seconds later: 'Third buttress. Western wall. NOW.' The player is directed to Step 6 regardless.

steps.unlock_message: Le Pharmacien has dropped a document into your vault. The monastery ledger changes everything — Vera's map isn't invention, it's reconstruction. But reconstruction from what? The tower holds the answer in its stones.

steps.title: The Mask Unmade

activity.name: Musée des Explorations du Monde — Interior Investigation & Final Cipher Resolution
activity.address: Place de la Castre, Le Suquet, 06400 Cannes

steps.narrative_intro:
The heavy wooden door closes behind you and the noise of Le Suquet — the gulls, the distant hum of the port — cuts to silence. The air inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde smells of old stone and polished wood, centuries folded into glass cases. You're standing in the castle that once watched over every ship entering Cannes harbor, built by monks from the same Lérins monastery that produced the reliquary that started all of this.

Your phone vibrates. Three messages arrive simultaneously — Vera, Noor, and Yael, all aware you've entered the museum. Each wants something different from the next few minutes. The cipher's final coordinate points to an object in the Mediterranean antiquities collection on the ground floor. A specific vessel. A specific inscription. The map was always leading here — to the place where the jailer hid something inside the one institution nobody would ever think to rob: a museum that didn't exist yet.

But Vera's cipher is a forgery. You know that now. Which means the coordinates she gave you point to something she planted in the narrative — not something the jailer hid. The real question isn't what the cipher says. It's what's actually here, in this collection, that Vera is so desperate for you to find. And why Le Pharmacien just sent you a photograph of a page from the prison registry of Fort Royal, dated 1698, with one entry circled: an inventory of items transferred with the masked prisoner. Item 7: "un vase de terre, contenant des papiers, scellé."

A sealed clay vessel containing papers. Transferred with the prisoner. Lost when Napoleon dismantled the fortress archives.

You're looking for a Provençal clay vessel in the Mediterranean antiquities collection. If it's here — if it was always here, miscatalogued, hiding in plain sight for three centuries — then Vera didn't forge the trail. She reverse-engineered it from real evidence. And the documents inside may have been read by the museum's curators without anyone understanding what they had.

The yacht in the Vieux Port isn't going anywhere. Yael's people have delayed its departure with a customs inspection. You have time. But Marco just went silent, and Noor's last message ended mid-sentence. Something has shifted. Find the vessel. Photograph it. And decide who gets to know what you find.
steps.instruction: Your ticket has been activated through the app. Enter the Musée des Explorations du Monde and make your way to the Mediterranean antiquities collection on the ground floor. You are looking for a Provençal clay vessel — likely terracotta, likely 17th century or catalogued as such — among the Mediterranean artifacts. Examine the vessels on display. Look for any that match the description: sealed or once-sealed, Provençal origin, roughly the size described in a prison transfer inventory. Photograph the vessel or vessels you believe could be the one referenced in the 1698 Fort Royal registry. Then climb the Tour du Suquet — the medieval watchtower — and from the panoramic viewing platform, photograph the Vieux Port below. Noor needs visual confirmation of the yacht's position, and Vera needs to know if a second vessel has appeared at the eastern dock. Send both photographs through the app. Your choices in the next few minutes determine who walks away with the evidence — and who walks away with nothing.
steps.player_action: Find and photograph a Provençal terracotta vessel in the Mediterranean antiquities collection that could match the 1698 Fort Royal prison transfer inventory description. Then climb the Tour du Suquet watchtower and photograph the panoramic view of the Vieux Port, identifying the yacht's position at the docks below.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution scan of a page from the 1698 Fort Royal prison transfer registry, with Item 7 circled in red: 'un vase de terre, contenant des papiers, scellé' — a sealed clay vessel containing papers. Marginal note in different handwriting reads: 'envoyé à la tour de Cannes, inventaire civil.' A second document accompanies it: a partial 19th-century museum acquisition log from the Château de la Castre showing 'divers objets de terre, provenance monastique, Lérins' — miscellaneous clay objects of monastic provenance from Lérins. Le Pharmacien's only annotation: 'It was never lost. It was filed wrong. Room 2, west wall. Go.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs terracotta or clay vessels in the Mediterranean antiquities collection. AI identifies the vessel type, approximate period, provenance information on museum labels, any visible seals or markings. The AI weaves what it sees into the narrative — matching or contrasting the vessel with the 1698 description. If the player photographs the panoramic view from the Tour du Suquet, AI identifies the Vieux Port, dock positions, and any large vessels visible, interpreting them as the seized yacht in the narrative context.

tension.description: All threads converge inside the museum. Vera's forgery is exposed but her trail proved real. Noor has gone silent — possibly intercepted by the buyer's network. Marco's betrayal or loyalty depends on what the player shared with him. Yael is demanding the player transmit everything to her as legal evidence. Le Pharmacien wants public disclosure. The player holds the pivot: who they send the photographs to first determines who controls the discovery. The vessel may actually contain — or have once contained — documents that identify the Man in the Iron Mask. Three centuries of mystery, sitting in a glass case labeled 'Provençal, 17th c., monastic origin, use unknown.'
tension.resolution: The player photographs the vessel and the view from the tower, then chooses who receives the evidence. The twist lands: Vera forged the cipher map, yes — but she forged it by working BACKWARD from real evidence she found in this museum five years ago. She was the anonymous researcher who requested access to the Lérins acquisition archive in 2021. The vessel was opened by conservators in 1987 and the papers inside were catalogued as 'liturgical fragments, untranslated.' They are still in the museum's archive. Vera has been engineering this entire day to force an official re-examination of those papers — because no one believed her when she published her findings. The reliquary, the auction, the cipher — all theater to create a public event dramatic enough that the documents couldn't be ignored again. The player can validate her scheme or expose it. Either way, the documents get a second look.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photograph of the terracotta vessel from the Mediterranean collection
character_interactions.phone_version:
Vera's response arrives after a long pause — longer than any of her previous messages. When it comes, her tone has shifted entirely. The performance is over.

'You found it. I know you found it because I found it five years ago. Same room, same case, same awful fluorescent lighting. I requested access to the Lérins acquisition archive in 2021 under a false academic credential. The conservators opened that vessel in 1987. The papers inside were catalogued as liturgical fragments — untranslated, unstudied, forgotten. I read them. They are not liturgical. They are personal letters, written in a cipher that matches the one used by Saint-Mars, the jailer of the Iron Mask. I published my findings in the Revue d'Histoire Moderne. No one read it. No one cared. So I built all of this — the reliquary, the auction, the cipher map, your involvement — to create a situation so dramatic that the academic world and the press would have to re-examine those papers. You were never authenticating a reliquary. You were authenticating a story. And now you have to decide: do you tell Yael everything, and I go to prison for fraud and forgery? Or do you validate the trail, the documents get examined properly, and the Man in the Iron Mask finally gets a name? I won't insult you by saying there's an easy answer. There isn't. But you've earned the right to make this call. Not Noor. Not Yael. You.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's voice comes through raw, stripped of its usual precision. 'You found it. Same room where I found it five years ago. I published what those papers contain. No one listened. So I fabricated a spectacle — the reliquary, the cipher, you — to force the world to look. Those papers are real. My methods are not. Now you decide who I am: a criminal, or the woman who solved history's greatest mystery and couldn't get anyone to care. Send the photo to Yael and I'm done. Send it to Le Pharmacien and the papers get their day. Your call. I'm tired of making it for everyone.'

contextual_music.track_type: minimal piano, single repeated note dissolving into silence

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photograph of the Vieux Port panorama from the Tour du Suquet
character_interactions.phone_version:
Yael's message is clipped, professional, but there's an undercurrent of urgency she's no longer hiding.

'Good. I can see the yacht from your angle — eastern berth, third slip. My team boarded twenty minutes ago under customs authority. The skeleton crew had already begun destroying digital records. We recovered partial files linking three shell companies to properties purchased with proceeds from the 2013 Carlton heist. This is the break the investigating magistrate has needed for eleven years. But I need more. If what you're telling me about Vera Solari is accurate — that she engineered a fraudulent auction to manipulate an investigation — that's a separate criminal matter. I need your testimony. I need the photographs. I need the vessel provenance. I understand she may have done this for reasons you find sympathetic. I find them sympathetic too. But I don't get to choose which laws apply based on how I feel about the person breaking them. Neither do you. Send me everything you have, and I will ensure the museum documents are examined through proper forensic channels. Or don't, and watch this become another conspiracy theory that lives and dies on Reddit. Your move, authenticator.'

rayban_version.script: Yael's voice is steady, deliberate — a captain who has waited years for this. 'I can see the yacht from your photo. My team is aboard. We've recovered partial Carlton heist files. Now I need you. If Vera engineered a fraud, I need your evidence. I know she may be right about those documents. But the law doesn't bend for good intentions. Send me what you have. I'll make sure the museum papers get a proper forensic examination. Or keep it to yourself and watch this disappear into myth. You've been an authenticator all day. Authenticate this: does the truth need permission to come out?'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes the panoramic view to identify the yacht position and dock layout, feeding details into Yael's tactical response

character_interactions.trigger: 90 seconds after the Vera interaction resolves
character_interactions.phone_version:
Noor's message arrives fragmented, as though typed in a hurry.

'Still here. Sorry for going dark — the buyer's people found my real name. Had to switch devices. Listen: I don't care about Vera's scheme or Yael's case. My father spent thirty years arguing that the Iron Mask was an Armenian diplomat named Davtyan, and the Sorbonne laughed him out of his career. He died believing he was right. If those papers in the museum prove him wrong, fine. If they prove him right, I need to be the one to publish. Not Vera. Not some government press office. Not a podcast. Me. His daughter. In a peer-reviewed journal with his name as co-author. That's all I've wanted since I walked into that auction. You don't owe me anything. But if you photograph the papers' reference number from the museum catalogue — just the accession number — I can file an official academic access request tonight. Vera gets her vindication. Yael gets her evidence. I get to finish my father's work. This is the only version where everyone wins. Please.'

rayban_version.script: Noor's voice is breathless, hurried. 'I had to switch devices — the buyer's people found me. Listen. I don't care about Vera or Yael. My father died trying to prove the Iron Mask was an Armenian diplomat. If those papers prove anything, I need to publish it. His name, a real journal. Just photograph the accession number from the museum catalogue. I can file an academic access request tonight. Everyone gets what they want. This is the version where nobody loses. Please.'

contextual_music.track_type: distant oud melody, single phrase repeated and fading

verification.target: A terracotta or clay vessel in the Mediterranean antiquities collection AND a panoramic photograph of the Vieux Port from the Tour du Suquet watchtower
verification.success_condition: Player photographs a plausible Provençal terracotta vessel from the museum collection showing period-appropriate characteristics, AND photographs the Vieux Port panorama from the tower showing the dock area
verification.success_reaction: Le Pharmacien sends a final message: 'The accession number is 1987.LER.044. The conservator's report noted "papiers pliés, encre ferrogallique, état fragile, langue non identifiée." Folded papers, iron gall ink, fragile condition, unidentified language. Three hundred and twenty-eight years in a glass case and nobody asked what language it was because nobody expected a clay pot from a monastery to contain state secrets. You did well today. Better than I expected. Better than any of them deserved. Publish or don't. But remember: I gave you the truth for free. Everyone else charged admission.' — The player receives maximum XP, full trust from all characters who received evidence, and a blockchain-minted certificate: 'Salt and Ashes — Authenticator, First Class.' The final narrative beat acknowledges the player's choices and reflects them back: who they sent photos to, who they trusted, what kind of authenticator they became.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player cannot find a matching vessel or doesn't climb the tower, Le Pharmacien sends the accession number anyway: 'You're overthinking it. Room 2, west wall, third case from the door. It has a crack along the rim where the conservators opened it in 1987. The view from the tower would have been nice — Saint-Mars could see every ship from up there. But the documents matter more than the view. Here's the number: 1987.LER.044. Do what you want with it.' The story resolves with slightly less dramatic impact but all narrative threads close. The player still makes the final choice of who receives the evidence.
verification.timeout_reaction: Dimitri breaks his silence with a rare double-sentence message: 'Museum closes at 6. You're not the type to let a clock beat you.' Thirty seconds later, Vera sends: 'If you're hesitating, that means you're taking it seriously. That's more than anyone else has done. Take your time. The papers waited three centuries. They can wait ten more minutes.'

steps.unlock_message: The door opens onto centuries of collected expeditions — masks, instruments, weapons, maps. Somewhere in this castle, a clay vessel holds the answer to a question that has haunted historians since 1698. Le Pharmacien's documents have just appeared in your vault. Room 2. West wall. The jailer's secret has been hiding in plain sight. Find it.
